title: Burns: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Burns
Apply clinical reasoning to Burns management

sections.title: Overview of Burns
sections.content:
**Burns**



**Key Points:**
• Superficial (1st degree): epidermis only, painful, red, no blisters (sunburn)
• Partial thickness (2nd degree): epidermis + dermis, blisters, painful, wet appearance
• Full thickness (3rd degree): through dermis, painless (nerve destruction), white/leathery/charred

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Superficial (1st degree): epidermis only, painful, red, no blisters (sunburn)
• Partial thickness (2nd degree): epidermis + dermis, blisters, painful, wet appearance

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Full thickness (3rd degree): through dermis, painless (nerve destruction), white/leathery/charred
• Rule of 9s: head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%
• Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA in first 24h (half in first 8h, half in next 16h) — Lactated Ringer's

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Burns:**

• Full thickness (3rd degree): through dermis, painless (nerve destruction), white/leathery/charred
• Rule of 9s: head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%
• Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA in first 24h (half in first 8h, half in next 16h) — Lactated Ringer's

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Burns?

options.text: Superficial (1st degree): epidermis only, painful, red, no blisters (sunburn)
options.explanation: Superficial (1st degree): epidermis only, painful, red, no blisters (sunburn)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Burns?

options.text: Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA in first 24h (half in first 8h, half in next 16h) — Lactated Ringer's
options.explanation: Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA in first 24h (half in first 8h, half in next 16h) — Lactated Ringer's

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Burns. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Partial thickness (2nd degree): epidermis + dermis, blisters, painful, wet appearance
options.explanation: Partial thickness (2nd degree): epidermis + dermis, blisters, painful, wet appearance

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
